title: Short title and commencement
verbatim:
(1) This Act may be cited as the Cyber Security Act 2024.
(2) This Act comes into operation on a date to be appointed by the Prime Minister by notification in the Gazette.
translation: Names the legislation as the Cyber Security Act 2024. The Prime Minister determines the commencement date by Gazette notification. The Act was gazetted on 26 June 2024 and came into force on 26 August 2024.

title: Interpretation
translation: Defines all key terms used throughout the Act. Critical definitions include: 'national critical information infrastructure' (NCII — systems whose disruption would have debilitating impact on essential services), 'cyber security incident' (unauthorized activities jeopardizing cyber security), 'cyber security service' (protective or responsive services including SOC monitoring, penetration testing, and incident response), 'NCII entity' (designated entity under s17), and 'NCII sector lead' (government body overseeing a sector). The definition of NCII is deliberately broad and functional rather than prescriptive.

title: Application of Act
verbatim:
(1) This Act applies throughout Malaysia.
(2) This Act applies to any person in respect of any national critical information infrastructure whether located in or outside Malaysia.
(3) Where any act or omission constituting an offence under this Act is committed outside Malaysia by any person, that person may be dealt with as if the offence had been committed in Malaysia.
translation: The Act has nationwide application and extends extraterritorially to any person in relation to NCII, whether the infrastructure is located in or outside Malaysia. Offences committed outside Malaysia can be prosecuted as if committed domestically, giving the Act broad jurisdictional reach to protect Malaysia's critical infrastructure regardless of where threats originate.

title: Prevailing law
verbatim: The provisions of this Act shall be in addition to, and not in derogation of, the provisions of any other written law.
translation: The Cyber Security Act 2024 operates alongside and supplements all existing legislation. It does not override or diminish the effect of other laws such as the Computer Crimes Act 1997, PDPA 2010, or sector-specific regulations. Entities must comply with both this Act and any other applicable written law simultaneously.

title: Functions of the Agency
translation: Establishes NACSA as Malaysia's national lead agency for all cyber security matters. Its functions span policy coordination, strategy development, standards-setting, threat monitoring and incident response, inter-agency coordination, R&D promotion, awareness building, NCII advisory, and international cooperation. The broad 'incidental powers' clause in subsection (2) gives NACSA flexibility to exercise any power necessary to fulfil these functions.

title: Appointment and powers of Chief Executive
verbatim:
(1) The Prime Minister shall appoint a Chief Executive of the National Cyber Security Agency.
(2) The Chief Executive shall be responsible for the management, administration and control of the Agency.
(3) The Chief Executive shall have such powers as may be necessary to carry out his functions under this Act.
(4) The Chief Executive may, in writing, delegate any of his functions, duties or powers to any officer of the Agency.
(5) The delegation under subsection (4) shall not preclude the Chief Executive from performing or exercising, at any time, the functions, duties or powers so delegated.
translation: The Prime Minister appoints the Chief Executive (CE) of NACSA, who is responsible for the agency's overall management and administration. The CE holds broad powers necessary for the Act's implementation and may delegate functions to NACSA officers in writing, while retaining the ability to exercise those delegated powers at any time. The CE's directives under s15 form a key regulatory instrument under the Act.

title: Appointment of officers and servants
verbatim:
(1) The Chief Executive may authorize in writing any officer of the Agency or any public officer to exercise the powers of enforcement under this Act.
(2) Such officers shall be subject to the direction and control of the Chief Executive.
(3) Any person authorized under subsection (1) shall be deemed to be a public servant within the meaning of the Penal Code.
translation: The CE may authorize NACSA officers or public officers in writing to exercise enforcement powers under the Act. These authorized officers operate under the CE's direction and are deemed public servants (gaining protections and obligations under the Penal Code). This is the mechanism by which enforcement capacity is created for NCII inspections, investigations, and audits.

title: Establishment of the National Cyber Security Committee
translation: Creates the National Cyber Security Committee at the highest level of government, chaired by the Prime Minister. Membership includes key cabinet ministers (communications, defence, home affairs), the Chief Secretary to the Government, the NSC Secretary General, and the NACSA CE as Secretary. Up to five additional members may be appointed by the PM. This composition signals that cyber security is treated as a national security priority at the apex of government.

title: Functions of the Committee
translation: The Committee advises the government on cyber security policy, oversees strategy implementation, determines NCII sectors (listed in the Schedule), coordinates national-level incident response, and promotes inter-agency cooperation. It may establish sub-committees for specialized areas. Critically, the power to determine NCII sectors (subsection (c)) means the Committee can expand or modify the Schedule's 11 sectors over time.

title: Delegation of functions
verbatim:
(1) The Committee may delegate any of its functions to any sub-committee established under section 9.
(2) Any delegation under subsection (1) shall not preclude the Committee from performing or exercising the functions so delegated.
translation: The Committee may delegate functions to sub-committees while retaining the right to exercise those functions itself. This allows the Committee to distribute operational cyber security oversight while maintaining strategic control and accountability at the Prime Ministerial level.

title: Meetings of the Committee
verbatim:
(1) The Committee shall meet at such times and places as the Chairman may determine.
(2) The quorum for a meeting of the Committee shall be a majority of its members including the Chairman.
(3) Every member of the Committee present at a meeting of the Committee shall be entitled to one vote.
(4) If on any question to be determined by the Committee there is an equality of votes, the Chairman shall have a casting vote.
translation: Establishes procedural rules for Committee meetings. The PM as Chairman determines meeting schedule and venue. A majority of members including the Chairman forms a quorum. Each member has one vote, with the Chairman holding a casting vote in case of a tie.

title: Minutes of meetings
verbatim:
(1) Minutes of each meeting shall be kept and maintained by the Secretary.
(2) The minutes of meetings shall, if duly confirmed, be admissible in evidence in all legal proceedings without further proof.
translation: The CE (as Secretary) must keep meeting minutes, which are admissible as evidence in legal proceedings once confirmed. This creates an authoritative record of the Committee's decisions, particularly regarding NCII sector determinations and policy directions.

title: Procedure at meetings
verbatim: Subject to this Act, the Committee may regulate its own procedure.
translation: Grants the Committee autonomy to determine its own meeting procedures, subject to the Act's provisions.

title: Disclosure of interest
verbatim:
(1) A member of the Committee who has or acquires, directly or indirectly, any interest in any matter under discussion by the Committee shall disclose to the Committee the fact of his interest and the nature of that interest.
(2) A disclosure under subsection (1) shall be recorded in the minutes of the meeting of the Committee.
(3) After a disclosure under subsection (1), the member shall not be present or take part in any deliberation or decision of the Committee relating to the matter.
translation: Mandatory conflict-of-interest provisions for Committee members. Any direct or indirect interest in a matter must be disclosed, recorded in minutes, and the member must then recuse themselves from related deliberations and decisions. This is critical governance safeguard given the Committee's power to designate NCII entities and sectors.

title: Chief Executive may issue directives
verbatim:
(1) The Chief Executive may, from time to time, issue directives, not inconsistent with the provisions of this Act, as may be necessary or expedient for carrying out the purposes of this Act.
(2) Every NCII entity and NCII sector lead shall comply with the directives issued under subsection (1).
(3) A directive issued under subsection (1) shall be published in the Gazette.
translation: Empowers the CE to issue directives that have binding force on all NCII entities and sector leads. Directives must be consistent with the Act and published in the Gazette. This is a key regulatory tool — the CE has issued approximately 10 directives covering NCII registration, risk assessment methodology, audit standards, incident notification, and other operational matters. Non-compliance with directives is enforceable under the Act.

title: Secretariat to the Committee
verbatim: The Agency shall provide the secretariat to the Committee and such administrative support as may be necessary for the performance of the Committee's functions.
translation: NACSA provides secretariat and administrative support to the National Cyber Security Committee, ensuring the operational linkage between the strategic oversight body and the implementing agency.

title: Designation of NCII entity and NCII sector lead
verbatim:
(1) The Chief Executive may, by written notice, designate any person who owns or operates a national critical information infrastructure as an NCII entity.
(2) The Chief Executive may designate a Government Entity as an NCII sector lead for an NCII sector.
(3) Before making a designation under subsection (1), the Chief Executive shall consider—
(a) the nature and extent of the infrastructure;
(b) the consequences that may arise if the infrastructure is compromised; and
(c) any other matter as the Chief Executive considers relevant.
(4) The Chief Executive shall notify the person designated under subsection (1) of the designation by written notice.
(5) A person aggrieved by a designation under subsection (1) may appeal to the Minister within thirty days of being notified.
translation: The CE may designate any owner or operator of NCII as an NCII entity by written notice, considering the infrastructure's nature, extent, and consequences of compromise. A Government Entity may be designated as the sector lead for each NCII sector. The designated entity must be notified in writing and has 30 days to appeal to the Minister. This is the gateway provision — once designated, an entity becomes subject to all Part IV obligations including risk assessments, audits, standards compliance, and incident notification.

title: Duties of NCII entity
translation: Establishes the comprehensive duty catalogue for designated NCII entities. Entities must: maintain cyber security measures for NCII protection, comply with all Act-related standards/codes/directives, report incidents, conduct risk assessments and audits, and provide information when required. Subsection (2) creates a serious criminal offence for non-compliance — up to RM500,000 fine and/or 10 years imprisonment. This is the most consequential provision for NCII entities as it aggregates all core obligations into a single enforceable duty.

title: Duties of NCII sector lead
translation: Sector leads (government entities overseeing each of the 11 NCII sectors) must coordinate and monitor NCII cyber security within their sector, develop sector-specific codes of practice, advise and assist NCII entities, and report to the CE on sectoral cyber security posture. They may also issue sector-specific guidelines. For example, Bank Negara Malaysia as sector lead for Banking and Finance develops CoPs and advises banking NCII entities on their Act obligations.

title: Provision of information
verbatim:
(1) The Chief Executive may, by written notice, require an NCII entity to provide any information or document relating to the national critical information infrastructure of the NCII entity within a specified period.
(2) An NCII entity shall comply with a notice under subsection (1).
(3) Any person who fails to comply with a notice under subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years or to both.
(4) Any person who, in complying with a notice under subsection (1), provides any information or document that he knows or has reason to believe is false or misleading commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years or to both.
translation: The CE may compel NCII entities to provide information or documents about their NCII within a specified timeframe. Non-compliance carries penalties of up to RM200,000 and/or 3 years imprisonment. Providing false or misleading information is a separate, more serious offence (RM300,000 and/or 5 years). This gives NACSA investigative and oversight power to assess the cyber security posture of critical infrastructure without needing to conduct a full audit or investigation.

title: Cyber security risk assessment
verbatim:
(1) An NCII entity shall conduct or cause to be conducted a cyber security risk assessment of its national critical information infrastructure—
(a) at intervals of not more than such period as may be prescribed; and
(b) within such period as may be specified, upon being directed by the Chief Executive to do so.
(2) A cyber security risk assessment under subsection (1) shall be conducted by a person approved by the Chief Executive.
(3) The NCII entity shall submit the results of the cyber security risk assessment to the Chief Executive and to its NCII sector lead within such period as may be prescribed.
(4) An NCII entity that fails to comply with this section commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years or to both.
translation: NCII entities must conduct periodic cyber security risk assessments (frequency prescribed by regulation, typically annually) and also on-demand when directed by the CE. Assessments must be conducted by CE-approved persons (e.g., licensed cyber security service providers). Results must be submitted to both the CE and the relevant sector lead within prescribed timeframes. Non-compliance carries severe penalties (RM500,000/10 years). The Cyber Security (Risk Assessment and Audit) Regulations 2024 provides detailed methodology and frequency requirements.

title: Codes of practice
verbatim:
(1) The Chief Executive may, on the recommendation of the NCII sector lead, approve codes of practice for any NCII sector on standards of performance, procedures, and best practices in relation to the cyber security of national critical information infrastructure.
(2) An NCII entity shall comply with any code of practice approved under subsection (1) that applies to it.
(3) The Chief Executive may vary, revoke, or replace any code of practice approved under subsection (1).
(4) An NCII entity that fails to comply with any code of practice approved under subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years or to both.
translation: The CE approves sector-specific codes of practice (CoPs) on the recommendation of sector leads. CoPs set standards of performance, procedures, and best practices for NCII cyber security. NCII entities must comply with applicable CoPs, and failure to do so is an offence (RM200,000/3 years). The CE may vary or replace CoPs as cyber threats evolve. Each of the 11 NCII sectors is expected to have its own CoP, though development status varies.

title: Cyber security audit
verbatim:
(1) An NCII entity shall conduct or cause to be conducted a cyber security audit of its national critical information infrastructure—
(a) at intervals of not more than such period as may be prescribed; and
(b) within such period as may be specified, upon being directed by the Chief Executive to do so.
(2) A cyber security audit under subsection (1) shall be conducted by a person approved by the Chief Executive.
(3) The NCII entity shall submit the results of the cyber security audit to the Chief Executive and to its NCII sector lead within such period as may be prescribed.
(4) An NCII entity that fails to comply with this section commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years or to both.
translation: Parallel to s21 (risk assessment), NCII entities must conduct periodic cyber security audits (prescribed frequency) and on-demand audits when directed by the CE. Audits must be performed by CE-approved persons and results submitted to the CE and sector lead within prescribed timeframes. Same penalty regime as risk assessments (RM500,000/10 years). The audit assesses compliance with the Act, regulations, directives, codes of practice, and applicable standards.

title: Standards of cyber security
verbatim:
(1) The Chief Executive may determine standards of cyber security to be complied with by NCII entities in relation to the protection of national critical information infrastructure.
(2) An NCII entity shall comply with any standard determined under subsection (1) within such period as may be specified by the Chief Executive.
(3) An NCII entity that fails to comply with any standard determined under subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years or to both.
translation: The CE may set binding cyber security standards for NCII protection. NCII entities must comply within specified timeframes, with non-compliance carrying penalties of up to RM200,000 and/or 3 years imprisonment. Standards may reference or adapt international frameworks (ISO 27001, NIST CSF, etc.) and provide the technical baseline that audits measure against.

title: Duty to provide information on threats
verbatim:
(1) If an NCII entity has any information relating to a cyber security threat or cyber security incident that may affect any other national critical information infrastructure, the NCII entity shall inform the Chief Executive of such information as soon as practicable.
(2) An NCII entity that fails to comply with subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years or to both.
translation: Creates a mandatory threat intelligence sharing obligation. If an NCII entity has information about a threat or incident that could affect other NCII (not just its own), it must inform the CE as soon as practicable. This enables early warning across sectors and supports NACSA's coordination role. Non-compliance: RM200,000/3 years. The duty extends beyond an entity's own infrastructure to the broader national NCII ecosystem.

title: Notification of cyber security incident
verbatim:
(1) If an NCII entity becomes aware of a cyber security incident in respect of its national critical information infrastructure, the NCII entity shall notify the Chief Executive of the cyber security incident—
(a) within six hours after the NCII entity becomes aware of the cyber security incident, where the incident has a significant impact; or
(b) within such other period as may be prescribed.
(2) A notification under subsection (1) shall contain such information as may be prescribed.
(3) An NCII entity that fails to comply with subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years or to both.
(4) An NCII entity that provides any false or misleading information in a notification under subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years or to both.
(5) A continuing offence under subsection (3) shall be liable, in addition, to a daily fine not exceeding five thousand ringgit for each day the offence continues after conviction.
translation: The core incident notification provision. NCII entities must notify the CE of cyber security incidents affecting their NCII within 6 hours of awareness (for significant-impact incidents) or other prescribed periods. The notification must contain prescribed information (detailed in the Notification Regulations). Non-notification carries the heaviest penalties in Part IV (RM500,000/10 years), with a continuing daily penalty of RM5,000. Providing false notification information is separately criminalised (RM300,000/5 years). This is analogous to PDPA's breach notification but with a much shorter timeline (6 hours vs 72 hours for PDPA).

title: Chief Executive may share information
verbatim:
(1) The Chief Executive may share any information received under this Part with—
(a) any NCII sector lead;
(b) any NCII entity;
(c) any Government Entity; or
(d) any foreign country or international organization,
for the purpose of protecting the national critical information infrastructure.
(2) The Chief Executive shall ensure that any information shared under subsection (1) is used only for the purpose for which it is shared.
translation: Authorizes the CE to share information received under Part IV (including incident reports, threat intelligence, and risk assessment results) with sector leads, other NCII entities, government entities, and international partners — but only for NCII protection purposes. Includes a purpose limitation safeguard. This enables NACSA's coordination and early-warning functions while protecting information confidentiality.

title: Licensing of cyber security service providers
verbatim:
(1) No person shall provide any cyber security service unless the person holds a valid licence granted under this Act.
(2) An application for a licence shall be made to the Chief Executive in such form and manner as may be prescribed.
(3) The Chief Executive may, upon receiving an application under subsection (2), grant or refuse to grant the licence.
(4) A licence granted under this section shall be subject to such conditions as the Chief Executive may impose.
(5) Any person who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years or to both.
translation: Creates a mandatory licensing regime for cyber security service providers. No person may provide cyber security services (defined in s2 to include SOC monitoring, penetration testing, and incident response) without a valid CE-issued licence. Applications are made in prescribed form; the CE may impose licence conditions. Unlicensed provision is a serious criminal offence (RM500,000/10 years). The Cyber Security (Licensing) Regulations 2024 prescribe detailed application procedures, qualifications, and conditions.

title: Duration and renewal of licence
verbatim:
(1) A licence granted under section 28 shall be valid for a period of three years from the date of grant unless sooner revoked or suspended.
(2) A licence may be renewed upon application made to the Chief Executive not less than thirty days before the expiry of the licence.
(3) The provisions of section 28 shall apply to an application for renewal as they apply to an application for the grant of a licence.
translation: Licences are valid for 3 years and renewable. Renewal applications must be submitted at least 30 days before expiry, following the same process as initial applications. Service providers must plan for timely renewal to avoid gaps in licensed status.

title: Suspension and revocation of licence
translation: The CE may suspend or revoke a licence for: contravening the Act or licence conditions, fraud or dishonesty convictions, obtaining the licence by misrepresentation, ceasing business operations, or corporate winding up. Procedural fairness is required — the licensee must receive written notice and an opportunity to make representations before suspension/revocation. A 30-day appeal right to the Minister exists.

title: Register of licensees
verbatim:
(1) The Chief Executive shall maintain a register of all persons who hold a licence granted under this Part.
(2) The register shall contain such particulars as may be prescribed.
(3) The register or any extract thereof, certified by the Chief Executive, shall be admissible as prima facie evidence.
translation: The CE maintains a public register of licensed cyber security service providers. The register contents are prescribed by regulation and certified extracts are admissible as prima facie evidence. This enables NCII entities to verify that their service providers (e.g., auditors, penetration testers) hold valid licences.

title: Obligations of licensee
translation: Licensed providers must comply with licence conditions, maintain professional standards (competence, integrity, professionalism), preserve client confidentiality, keep service records, and follow CE directives/guidelines. Non-compliance is a criminal offence. The confidentiality obligation is particularly important given that licensed providers may have deep access to NCII entities' systems and sensitive security information.

title: Effect of suspension or revocation
verbatim:
(1) Where a licence is suspended or revoked, the licensee shall immediately cease to provide any cyber security service.
(2) The suspension or revocation of a licence shall not affect any liability incurred before the date of the suspension or revocation.
translation: Upon suspension or revocation, a licensee must immediately stop providing cyber security services. However, liabilities incurred before the suspension/revocation (e.g., contractual obligations, penalties for past non-compliance) remain enforceable.

title: Power of investigation
verbatim:
(1) An authorized officer may investigate any offence under this Act.
(2) For the purpose of any investigation under subsection (1), an authorized officer may exercise the special powers in relation to police investigation in the Criminal Procedure Code.
translation: Authorized officers may investigate any offence under the Act and may exercise the special powers of police investigation under the Criminal Procedure Code. This gives NACSA enforcement officers significant investigative authority equivalent to police powers.

title: Search and seizure with warrant
translation: Magistrates may issue search warrants where there is reasonable cause to believe premises contain evidence of Act offences. Authorized officers may enter by force, break open doors/windows, remove obstructions, and detain persons during the search. They may seize computers, systems, data, documents, and other relevant articles.

title: Search and seizure without warrant
verbatim: (1) An authorized officer may, without a warrant, enter any premises and exercise all the powers referred to in section 35 if he has reasonable cause to believe that by reason of delay in obtaining a search warrant the investigation would be adversely affected, evidence would be tampered with, or the object of the search would be frustrated.
translation: Allows warrantless search and seizure where delay in obtaining a warrant would adversely affect the investigation, risk evidence tampering, or frustrate the search objective. The same powers as warrant-based searches apply.

title: Access to computerized data
verbatim:
(1) An authorized officer conducting a search under this Act shall be given access to computerized data whether stored in a computer or otherwise.
(2) For the purposes of this section, an authorized officer shall be provided with the necessary password, encryption code, decryption code, software, or hardware or any other means required to enable comprehension of computerized data.
translation: Authorized officers must be given access to computerized data during searches. The entity must provide passwords, encryption/decryption codes, software, hardware, or any other means needed to access and comprehend the data. Failure to provide access could constitute obstruction under s44.

title: Power to intercept communications
verbatim:
(1) Notwithstanding any other written law, the Chief Executive or any authorized officer authorized in writing by the Chief Executive may, if he considers that it is likely to contain information relevant to the investigation of an offence under this Act, intercept or listen to any communication.
(2) Any evidence obtained through interception under subsection (1) shall be admissible in any proceedings under this Act.
translation: The CE or CE-authorized officers may intercept or listen to communications if likely to contain information relevant to an investigation under the Act. This is a broad surveillance power that overrides other legislation. Intercepted evidence is admissible in proceedings. This power is subject to administrative safeguards (written authorization by the CE) but does not require judicial authorization.

title: List of things seized
verbatim:
(1) An authorized officer making a search under this Act shall prepare a list of all things seized and of the premises in which they are respectively found.
(2) The list shall be signed by the authorized officer and by the occupier or his agent.
(3) A copy of the list shall be delivered to the occupier or his agent.
translation: A detailed inventory of all seized items must be prepared, signed by both the authorized officer and the premises occupier (or agent), and a copy delivered to the occupier. This provides accountability and an evidentiary chain of custody for seized items.

title: Power to require attendance of persons
verbatim:
(1) An authorized officer making an investigation under this Act may, by notice in writing, require any person who appears to be acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case to attend before him.
(2) Such person shall attend as required and shall answer all questions relating to the case truthfully.
(3) Any person who fails to attend without reasonable excuse commits an offence.
translation: Authorized officers may compel any person with knowledge of the case to attend for questioning. The person must attend and answer truthfully. Failure to attend without reasonable excuse is a criminal offence.

title: Examination of persons
verbatim:
(1) An authorized officer may examine orally any person supposed to be acquainted with the facts and circumstances of any offence under this Act.
(2) Such person shall be legally bound to answer all questions relating to such case put to him by the authorized officer.
(3) A statement made by any person under this section shall be reduced into writing and signed by the person, and may be used as evidence in any proceedings under this Act.
translation: Authorized officers may orally examine any person with knowledge of an offence. The person is legally bound to answer all questions. Statements are reduced to writing, signed, and admissible as evidence. This provides NACSA with powerful investigative tools comparable to those available to other enforcement agencies.

title: Admissibility of statements
verbatim:
(1) In any trial or inquiry by a court into any offence under this Act, any statement made by any person to an authorized officer in the course of an investigation under this Act shall be admissible in evidence.
(2) Nothing in subsection (1) shall render admissible any statement made by a person under arrest.
translation: Statements made during investigations are admissible as evidence in court proceedings, except statements made by persons under arrest (to protect the right against self-incrimination).

title: Power of arrest
verbatim:
(1) An authorized officer may arrest without warrant any person whom he reasonably believes has committed or is committing any offence under this Act.
(2) An authorized officer making an arrest shall, without unnecessary delay, bring the person arrested before the nearest Magistrate.
translation: Authorized officers may arrest without warrant any person reasonably believed to have committed or be committing an offence under the Act. Arrested persons must be brought before the nearest Magistrate without unnecessary delay, in accordance with constitutional protections.

title: Obstruction
translation: Criminalises obstruction of authorized officers, including refusing access, assaulting/obstructing/hindering/delaying officers, refusing to produce documents or data, and refusing to answer questions. Penalty: RM300,000/5 years. This is a significant enforcement provision that ensures NACSA officers can effectively exercise their powers.

title: Seizure of movable property
verbatim:
(1) An authorized officer may seize any movable property which he reasonably suspects to be evidence of or connected with any offence under this Act.
(2) A list of all property seized shall be prepared and a copy delivered to the person from whom the property was seized.
translation: Officers may seize any movable property reasonably suspected to be evidence or connected to an offence. An inventory must be prepared and provided to the person from whom the property was seized.

title: Release of seized property
verbatim:
(1) Any property seized under this Act shall be liable to forfeiture.
(2) Any property seized may be returned to the person from whom it was seized if the Chief Executive is satisfied that it is not required for the purpose of any proceedings under this Act.
(3) A person aggrieved by the seizure of any property may apply to the court for its release.
translation: Seized property is liable to forfeiture but may be returned if the CE determines it is not needed for proceedings. Aggrieved persons may apply to court for release of seized property.

title: Cost of holding seized property
verbatim: Where any property seized under this Act is held in the custody of the Government pending the completion of any proceedings in respect of an offence under this Act, the cost of maintaining such property in custody shall be a charge on the property and may be recovered from the person convicted of the offence.
translation: The cost of maintaining seized property in government custody is charged to the property and may be recovered from the convicted person. This prevents the government from bearing storage costs for large quantities of seized equipment.

title: Appeal to the Minister
verbatim:
(1) Any person aggrieved by any decision of the Chief Executive under this Act may appeal to the Minister within thirty days of being notified of the decision.
(2) The Minister may, after considering the appeal, confirm, vary or reverse the decision of the Chief Executive.
(3) The decision of the Minister on an appeal under this section shall be final.
translation: Creates a general right of appeal to the Minister against any CE decision, with a 30-day filing window. The Minister may confirm, vary, or reverse the CE's decision, and the Minister's decision is final (no further administrative appeal, though judicial review may still be available). This covers appeals against NCII designation, licence refusal/revocation, and other CE decisions.

title: Obligation of secrecy
verbatim:
(1) Any person who has any information acquired in the course of performing any function or exercising any power under this Act shall not disclose that information to any person except—
(a) for the purpose of this Act;
(b) for the purpose of any civil or criminal proceedings under this Act or any other written law; or
(c) with the written consent of the Chief Executive.
(2) Any person who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years or to both.
translation: Imposes a strict secrecy obligation on anyone who acquires information in the course of performing functions under the Act. Disclosure is permitted only for Act purposes, for legal proceedings, or with the CE's written consent. Breach carries severe penalties (RM500,000/10 years). This protects sensitive NCII information, threat intelligence, and investigation details from unauthorized disclosure.

title: Protection of informers
verbatim:
(1) Except as provided in subsection (3), no complaint as to an offence under this Act shall be admitted in evidence in any civil or criminal proceedings, and no witness shall be obliged to disclose the name or address of any informer or the substance of the information received from the informer.
(2) No officer of the Agency shall be compelled to disclose the source of any information.
(3) If any evidence is given by any person that the informer is a person who has participated in an offence, the court may require full disclosure.
translation: Protects the identity of informers who report offences under the Act. No witness or NACSA officer is compelled to disclose informer identity unless the court determines the informer participated in the offence. This encourages reporting of cyber security violations and threats.

title: Evidence of agent provocateur
verbatim:
(1) Notwithstanding any written law or rule of law to the contrary, no agent provocateur shall be presumed to be unworthy of credit by reason only of his having attempted to commit or abet or having abetted or having been engaged in a criminal conspiracy to commit the offence.
(2) Any statement made by an agent provocateur shall be admissible as evidence.
translation: Agent provocateur evidence is admissible and such persons are not presumed unworthy of credit merely because of their involvement in the offence. This supports undercover operations against cyber criminals targeting NCII.

title: Offences by body corporate
verbatim:
(1) If any offence under this Act is committed by a body corporate, any person who at the time of the commission of the offence was a director, chief executive officer, chief operating officer, manager, secretary or other similar officer of the body corporate, or was purporting to act in any such capacity, or was in any manner or to any extent responsible for the management of any of the affairs of the body corporate, or was assisting in such management—
(a) may be charged severally or jointly in the same proceedings with the body corporate; and
(b) if the body corporate is found to have committed the offence, shall be deemed to have committed that offence unless he proves that the offence was committed without his knowledge, consent or connivance, and that he had exercised all due diligence to prevent the commission of the offence.
(2) If the body corporate is convicted of the offence, the court may impose on the body corporate a fine of not more than ten times the maximum fine to which the body corporate would have been liable.
translation: When a body corporate commits an offence, directors, CEO, COO, managers, secretaries, and other officers may be personally charged. There is a reverse burden of proof — officers are deemed to have committed the offence unless they prove it occurred without their knowledge/consent/connivance and that they exercised due diligence. Additionally, courts may impose fines up to 10 times the maximum on body corporates. This creates significant personal liability for corporate leadership and dramatically increases the financial exposure for corporate entities.

title: Prosecution
verbatim: No prosecution for any offence under this Act shall be instituted except with the written consent of the Public Prosecutor.
translation: All prosecutions under the Act require written consent of the Public Prosecutor, providing a safeguard against frivolous or politically motivated prosecutions and ensuring consistency in enforcement.

title: Compounding of offences
verbatim:
(1) The Chief Executive may, with the written consent of the Public Prosecutor, compound any offence under this Act which has been prescribed to be a compoundable offence.
(2) The amount of compound shall not exceed fifty per centum of the maximum fine for that offence.
(3) Upon the receipt of the compound, the person shall be released and no further proceedings shall be taken against him in respect of the offence.
translation: The CE may compound (settle out of court) prescribed offences with Public Prosecutor consent. The compound amount is capped at 50% of the maximum fine. Once compounded, no further proceedings may be taken. This provides an administrative resolution mechanism that avoids lengthy court proceedings for less serious violations. The Cyber Security (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2024 specifies which offences are compoundable.

title: Protection against suits and legal proceedings
verbatim:
No action, suit, prosecution or other proceedings shall lie or be brought, instituted or maintained in any court against—
(a) the Chief Executive, any authorized officer or any other person acting under this Act; or
(b) any person acting in compliance with any directive, notice, order or instruction issued under this Act,
for any act or omission done in good faith in the performance of any function or the exercise of any power under this Act.
translation: Provides legal immunity to the CE, authorized officers, and any person acting in good faith under the Act. Persons complying with directives, notices, or instructions under the Act are also protected. This shields officials and compliant entities from civil suits arising from good-faith exercise of their duties and powers.

title: Saving provisions
verbatim:
(1) Any act, decision, or order made, or any licence, permit or authority granted, under any written law relating to cyber security before the commencement of this Act shall, so far as it is consistent with this Act, continue in force until revoked or replaced.
(2) Any investigation, proceeding or other action commenced before the date of commencement of this Act may be continued as if this Act had not been enacted.
translation: Preserves the validity of pre-existing cyber security-related acts, decisions, licences, and permits insofar as they are consistent with the new Act. Investigations and proceedings that were underway before commencement continue under the prior legal framework. This prevents a regulatory vacuum during the transition period.

title: Transitional provision for NCII entities
verbatim:
(1) Any person who, immediately before the commencement of this Act, was operating or managing a national critical information infrastructure shall, within such period as may be prescribed, register with the Chief Executive.
(2) Until such person is designated as an NCII entity under section 17, the person shall be deemed to be an NCII entity for the purposes of this Act.
translation: Existing NCII operators must register with the CE within a prescribed period after commencement. Until formal designation under s17, they are deemed to be NCII entities — meaning they immediately bear all Part IV obligations. This ensures no gap in NCII protection during the transition from the pre-Act regime.

title: Transitional provision for cyber security service providers
verbatim:
(1) Any person who, immediately before the commencement of this Act, was providing a cyber security service shall, within such period as may be prescribed, apply for a licence under section 28.
(2) Until such application is determined, the person shall be deemed to hold a valid licence for the purposes of this Act.
translation: Existing cyber security service providers must apply for a licence within the prescribed period. Until their application is determined, they are deemed licensed — allowing continued operations without disruption while formal licensing is processed.

title: Transitional — pending proceedings
verbatim: Any investigation, inquiry, trial, or proceedings in any court commenced or pending immediately before the date of commencement of this Act in respect of any offence related to cyber security shall, after such date, be continued under the relevant written law as if this Act had not been enacted.
translation: Pending legal proceedings related to cyber security offences continue under the law that applied when they were commenced, not under the new Act. This ensures legal certainty and fairness for ongoing cases.

title: Power to make regulations
translation: The Minister may make regulations on any matter needed to carry out the Act, specifically covering forms, fees, risk assessment/audit procedures, incident notification requirements, licensing conditions, compoundable offences, and cyber security service classes. Regulations may create offences with penalties up to RM300,000 and/or 5 years. This is the enabling provision for the four subsidiary regulations gazetted alongside the Act.

title: Amendment of section 5 of the Computer Crimes Act 1997
verbatim:
The Computer Crimes Act 1997 [Act 563] is amended in section 5 by substituting for subsection (2) the following subsection:
'(2) If the offence is committed against any national critical information infrastructure as defined in the Cyber Security Act 2024 [Act 854], the offender shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding fifteen years or to both.'
translation: Amends the Computer Crimes Act 1997 s5 (unauthorized modification of computer contents) to create an enhanced penalty where the offence targets NCII: RM1,000,000 and/or 15 years imprisonment (significantly higher than the standard penalty). This aligns the criminal law framework with NCII protection priorities.

title: Amendment of section 6 of the Computer Crimes Act 1997
verbatim:
The Computer Crimes Act 1997 is amended in section 6 by substituting for subsection (2) the following subsection:
'(2) If the offence is committed against any national critical information infrastructure as defined in the Cyber Security Act 2024, the offender shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding fifteen years or to both.'
translation: Amends the Computer Crimes Act 1997 s6 (wrongful communication of access codes) to create enhanced penalties when the offence affects NCII: RM1,000,000 and/or 15 years. Targets insider threats and credential sharing that could compromise critical infrastructure.

title: Amendment of section 7 of the Computer Crimes Act 1997
verbatim:
The Computer Crimes Act 1997 is amended in section 7 by substituting for subsection (2) the following subsection:
'(2) If the offence is committed against any national critical information infrastructure as defined in the Cyber Security Act 2024, the offender shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million five hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding twenty years or to both.'
translation: Amends the Computer Crimes Act 1997 s7 (unauthorized interception) with the highest enhanced penalty tier for NCII-related offences: RM1,500,000 and/or 20 years imprisonment. Reflects the severity of intercepting communications related to critical national infrastructure.

title: New section 8A of the Computer Crimes Act 1997
verbatim:
The Computer Crimes Act 1997 is amended by inserting after section 8 the following section:
'8A. Cyber security offences against national critical information infrastructure
(1) Any person who knowingly causes or attempts to cause a cyber security incident against any national critical information infrastructure as defined in the Cyber Security Act 2024 commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding two million ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding twenty years or to both.
(2) For the purposes of this section, national critical information infrastructure has the meaning assigned to it in the Cyber Security Act 2024.'
translation: Inserts a new offence (s8A) into the Computer Crimes Act 1997: knowingly causing or attempting to cause a cyber security incident against NCII. This carries the heaviest penalties in the entire framework — RM2,000,000 and/or 20 years imprisonment. This creates a dedicated criminal offence for deliberate attacks on Malaysia's critical infrastructure, separate from general computer crimes.
